elements.sent-2411-001: Seysen's Python package \mmgroup{ provides functionality for fast computations within the sporadic simple group $\MM$, the Monster.}
elements.sent-2411-002: The aim of this work is to present an \mmgroup{ database of maximal subgroups of $\MM$: for each conjugacy class $\mathcal{C}$ of maximal subgroups in $\MM$, we construct explicit group elements in \mmgroup{ and prove that these elements generate a group in~$\mathcal{C}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Our generators and the computations verifying correctness are available in accompanying code.
elements.sent-2411-004: The maximal subgroups of $\MM$ have been classified in a number of papers spanning several decades; our work constitutes an independent verification of these constructions.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also correct the claim that $\MM$ has a maximal subgroup $\psl{2 {59}$, and hence identify a new maximal subgroup $59{: 29$.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The classification of the maximal subgroups of the Monster group $\MM$, the largest sporadic simple group, is a result of decades of effort led primarily by Holmes, Norton, and Wilson.
elements.sent-2411-007: In 2017, Wilson \cite{survey noted that over $15$ papers had been dedicated to this classification, yet a few open cases cases seemed largely resistant to theoretical arguments and posed significant computational difficulties.}
elements.sent-2411-008: These cases were recently settled by Dietrich, Lee, and Popiel \cite{dlp with the help of the newly available Python software package \mmgroup{ developed by Seysen \cite{serpent,fast_monster,mmgroup .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: For more background information and a comprehensive discussion, we refer to \cite{dlp and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-010: While much of the early classification work was theoretical, extensive computations eventually became necessary to construct or rule out the existence of various almost simple maximal subgroups.
elements.sent-2411-011: Several constructions, in fact, rely on calculations using a non-standard computational model developed by Holmes and Wilson \cite{monp .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Although groundbreaking at the time, this model is not publicly available, making it difficult to verify or reproduce the results.
elements.sent-2411-013: Seysen's \mmgroup{ package is a game changer in this regard, providing, for the first time, a publicly available framework for fast computations in~$\MM$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In the course of completing the maximal subgroup classification, the work in \cite{dlp yielded explicit generators in $\MM$ (in \mmgroup{ format) for the maximal subgroups $\mathrm{PGL _2(13)$ and $\mathrm{PSU _3(4)$, along with several auxiliary subgroups of $\MM$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The aim of this paper is to consider the remaining maximal subgroups of $\MM$, as described in the existing literature, and to provide explicit generators for these groups in \mmgroup{ format with computationally reproducible proofs of correctness.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Our generators (and computations verifying their correctness) are available in accompanying Python code \cite{pisgit .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Our explicit calculations in \mmgroup{ confirm the maximal subgroup constructions available in the literature, with the exception of the construction of $\psl{2 {59}$ in \cite{l2_59 ; this leads to our first result.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The Monster has no subgroup $\psl{2 {59}$, but a unique class of subgroups $59{: 29$, which are maximal subgroups.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Theorem \ref{thm_psl259 is in conflict with the main result of \cite{l2_59 which claims that $\MM$ has a maximal subgroup $\psl{2 {59}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: We prove Theorem \ref{thm_psl259 in Section \ref{sec_psl259 and illustrate in detail how the final conclusion of \cite{l2_59 is in contradiction with our calculations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Exhibiting elements in $\MM$ generating $59{: 29$ presents significant computational challenges and is the subject of ongoing work, see Remark \ref{remget59 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The main result of this paper can be summarised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-023: For each maximal subgroup $N$ of the Monster $\MM$ listed in Table \ref{maxsgtab , with the exception of $59{: 29$, the accompanying code \cite{pisgit provides explicit elements of $\MM$ in \mmgroup{ format that generate a maximal subgroup of $\MM$ isomorphic to $N$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: With the exception of the groups $\psl{2 {59}$ and $59{: 29$, which will be discussed in Section \ref{sec_psl259 , the correctness of this table follows from \cite{survey and \cite{dlp , see in particular \cite[Theorem~1.7]{dlp .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Generators for the maximal subgroups $\normTwoA$ and $\normTwoB$ are already provided in \cite{dlp and in \mmgroup{ .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: For each remaining maximal subgroup $N\ne 59{: 29$, we prove that the associated elements provided in the accompanying code \cite{pisgit generate a maximal subgroup of $\MM$ that is isomorphic to $N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Specifically, $p$-local maximal subgroups with $p$ odd are discussed in Section \ref{oddlocal .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The $2$-local maximal subgroups are discussed in Section \ref{2local .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Section \ref{a5s provides some necessary details on alternating subgroups $\alt{5}$ in $\MM$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Non-local maximal subgroups are considered in Sections \ref{psgl and \ref{nonlocal .}}

elements.sent-2411-031: This is a computational paper and the main result of our work is a database of maximal subgroups of $\MM$ in \mmgroup{ format, made available in a well-documented Jupyter Notebook \cite{pisgit .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: This paper is concerned with the proof that the database is correct.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our proof relies on theoretical results from the classification of the maximal subgroups, and on explicit computations in \mmgroup{ .}
elements.sent-2411-034: We also often refer to data concerning the maximal subgroups of various simple groups listed in the Atlas \cite{atlas,gap_ctbllib .}
elements.sent-2411-035: For each maximal subgroup $N\ne 59{: 29$ of $\MM$ listed in Table \ref{maxsgtab , there is a corresponding subsection in this paper and in the Jupyter Notebook.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We define certain elements of $\MM$ in \mmgroup{ format and prove that the group generated by these elements is maximal in $\MM$ and isomorphic to $N$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Depending on whether $N$ is $p$-local or not, we employ different strategies that are outlined below.. {\bf Notation.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Most of our notation is standard and typically aligns with the conventions established in the Atlas \cite{atlas .}
elements.sent-2411-039: One significant exception is that we use $\PSL_d(q)$ in place of $\mtt{L _d(q)$, and apply similar conventions for other simple classical groups.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Unless specified otherwise, we denote by $2\mt{A}$, $2\mt{B}$, $3\mt{A}$, etc.\ the conjugacy classes of $\MM$, and for a conjugacy class $p$X we write $p$X$^{k}$ for an elementary abelian group of size $p^k$ whose non-identity elements all belong to $p$X; such a group is called $p$X-{\em pure .}
elements.sent-2411-041: We denote the dihedral group of order $n$ by $\dih{n}$, the alternating group of degree $n$ by $\alt{n}$, and the symmetric group of degree $n$ by $\sym{n}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: The symbol $n$ is also used to denote a cyclic group of order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-043: An extension of a group $B$ by a group $A$ is denoted by $A.B$ (or occasionally $AB$), with $A$ being the normal subgroup.
elements.sent-2411-044: To emphasise that an extension is split, we may use the notation $A{: B$, whereas $A\udot B$ indicates a non-split extension.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We call this description of a group the shape of the group, and stress that the shape does in general not define the group up to isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-046: An elementary abelian group of order $p^k$ is denoted by $p^k$, where $p$ is a prime number and $k$ is a positive integer, while $p^{k+\ell}$ denotes an extension $p^k.p^\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-047: We often use a subscript to indicate the order of a group element; for instance, $g_5$ may refer to an element of order 5.
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, we denote by $\GG$ the centraliser $C_{\MM (z)$ of a distinguished 2B involution $z$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Extended functionality and faster computation are available in \mmgroup{ for elements of $\GG$; see \cite{fast_monster for details.}}

elements.sent-2411-051: We say that $U$ is $p$-local maximal if $U$ is $p$-local and also a maximal subgroup of $\MM$.
